macro_segments.title: Introduction to Beit Shammai and Beit Hillel debates on Shabbat; Overview of the 18 things

micro_segments.title: Context shift: moving from 18 things (Terumah u-Teharah) back to Shabbat-specific debates

macro_segments.title: Introduction to Beit Shammai and Beit Hillel debates on Shabbat; Overview of the 18 things (continued)

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Beit Shammai and Beit Hillel positions on soaking and dyeing materials before Shabbat

macro_segments.title: Beit Shammai's strict position: activities that cannot begin before Shabbat unless completed by day

micro_segments.title: Ink, dyes, vetch soaking; flax heating; wool dyeing restrictions
Traps for animals and birds cannot be set before Shabbat unless they will catch from day before
Selling to non-Jews and loading their donkeys; appearances and הקרוב concern
Giving leather to tanners and clothes to non-Jewish cleaners only if work completes by day

macro_segments.title: Agreement between Beit Shammai and Beit Hillel: lowering beams and weights on oil and wine vats

micro_segments.title: Both schools permit lowering vat beams and weights before Shabbat for continued pressing

macro_segments.title: Gemara's investigation into the melacha of לישה (kneading/absorbing) in the ink case

micro_segments.title: Who taught that putting water into ink or ash mixture constitutes sheriyatan (absorbing)?
Rebbi vs. Rebbi Yosei debate: is kneading necessary or does adding water suffice?
Abaiye's resolution: ink vs. flour; Rov Moshe application to mayonnaise and tuna

macro_segments.title: Braita on permitted activities before Shabbat: watering, incense, medicines, milling wheat

micro_segments.title: Opening water channels for irrigation; burning incense under clothes; applying salves
Exceptions: wheat in water mill requires completion by day because of noise (mashmi'a kol)

macro_segments.title: Gemara's exploration of שביתת כלים (rest of vessels) as Beit Shammai's underlying concern

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef proposes shevisas keilim as the reason for watermill restriction; Abaye's counter
Gemara conclusion: shevisas keilim is Beit Shammai's position; Beit Hillel disagrees
Alternative non-shevisas-keilim reading: Beit Shammai's concern is appearances (rabbinic), not biblical

macro_segments.title: Resolution of permitted uses: how food, lamps, and pots can cook/burn through Shabbat

micro_segments.title: If shevisas keilim is biblical, how do we permit lights and cooking? Answer: mafkir the vessels
Braita restriction: cannot put uncooked legumes in oven before Shabbat (fear of stirring coals)
Concern about stir: if food not fully cooked, fear you'll forget and adjust flames

macro_segments.title: Exceptions to blech requirement: raw meat and already-cooked food

micro_segments.title: Raw pot of meat can sit on flame without covering; fully cooked food also permitted
Only partially cooked food requires blech; you'll be anxious and stir to speed cooking
Raw bone or vegetable thrown into partially cooked dish: if long cooking time, no blech needed

macro_segments.title: Goat vs. ram meat; sealed vs. unsealed ovens; debate between Rav Ashi and Rav Yirmiyah

micro_segments.title: Goat meat in sealed oven: permissible (meat susceptible to air, oven sealed discourages opening)
Ram meat unsealed: problematic (nothing prevents opening door and stirring)
Debate: mixed cases (goat unsealed or ram sealed) disputed by Rav Ashi and Rav Yirmiyah
Resolution: slow coals on ground level (not in oven) present stirring risk; raw gourds like goat meat

macro_segments.title: Summary: three possible explanations for Beit Shammai; multiple concerns for cooking restrictions

micro_segments.title: Three layers: (1) appearances/rabbinic; (2) shevisas keilim biblical; (3) mashmi'a kol noise concern
Cooking concern: fear of stirring flames is primary; exceptions when no anxiety about cooking status

topical_tags.term: Appearances/הקרוב (looks like melacha is being done)
Stirring coals (concern you'll forget and stir)
Raw vs. partially cooked vs. fully cooked
